hypothesis.claim: Increasing MLP capacity (depth=2, hidden=256) with stronger L2 weight decay and a lower learning rate will improve generalization and reduce validation top-1 error below the 5.0 baseline.
hypothesis.source: L2 weight decay regularization (doi:10.1007/s10462-023-10466-8); ReLU/deeper MLP trainability (doi:10.1186/s40537-021-00444-8)
hypothesis.predicted_effect: Lower val_top1_err due to greater representational capacity balanced by regularization preventing overfitting.

reasoning.trace: No experiments have been run yet, so the priority is to establish a strong baseline with the tunable knobs available. The default depth=1 MLP with lr=0.05 may be underpowered and overfitting-prone. Literature priors point to L2 weight decay (doi:10.1007/s10462-023-10466-8) as a reliable regularizer to control model complexity, and a deeper network with more hidden units to improve capacity. Since edits to add dropout/batchnorm require a script rewrite and the advisor wants a strong baseline first, I'll start by tuning only the safe hyperparameters: increase depth to 2 and hidden to 256 for more capacity, slightly raise weight_decay to 0.0005 to curb overfitting, and lower lr to 0.02 for stable convergence over the 120 epochs. This is a conservative, well-grounded baseline move that should beat the 5.0 baseline.

votes.concern: This is a sensible first baseline establishing capacity/regularization on an empty ledger, but it bundles four simultaneous knob changes (depth, hidden, wd, lr) so the attribution will be muddy — fine for a first run, but the next iteration should isolate the high-leverage knobs (e.g., dropout/batchnorm) for bolder gains.

Multiple knobs (depth, hidden, lr, weight_decay) change simultaneously, so a result won't isolate which change drove the delta; acceptable as a first baseline run but follow up with one-factor sweeps.

This bundles four simultaneous changes (depth, hidden, weight_decay, lr) so a positive result won't isolate which knob drove the gain, but as the first run with an empty ledger establishing a strong baseline is reasonable and the move is low-risk.

concerns: Innovator: This is a sensible first baseline establishing capacity/regularization on an empty ledger, but it bundles four simultaneous knob changes (depth, hidden, wd, lr) so the attribution will be muddy — fine for a first run, but the next iteration should isolate the high-leverage knobs (e.g., dropout/batchnorm) for bolder gains.

Pragmatist: Multiple knobs (depth, hidden, lr, weight_decay) change simultaneously, so a result won't isolate which change drove the delta; acceptable as a first baseline run but follow up with one-factor sweeps.

Contrarian: This bundles four simultaneous changes (depth, hidden, weight_decay, lr) so a positive result won't isolate which knob drove the gain, but as the first run with an empty ledger establishing a strong baseline is reasonable and the move is low-risk.

hypothesis.claim: A wider, deeper MLP (hidden=256, depth=2) with stronger L2 weight decay and longer training will improve generalization and lower val_top1_err below the 5.0 baseline.
hypothesis.source: doi:10.1186/s40537-021-00444-8 (deeper ReLU MLPs improve trainability) and doi:10.1007/s10462-023-10466-8 (L2 weight decay reduces overfitting)
hypothesis.predicted_effect: Lower val_top1_err due to increased capacity balanced by regularization.

reasoning.trace: No experiments have been tried yet, so the priority is establishing a strong baseline with modern training tricks before architectural novelty. The default depth=1 MLP is shallow; literature (doi:10.1186/s40537-021-00444-8) supports deeper MLPs with ReLU and wider hidden layers improving capacity, while L2 weight decay (doi:10.1007/s10462-023-10466-8) controls overfitting. I will increase model capacity (hidden=256, depth=2) for richer representations, slightly raise weight_decay to 0.0005 for regularization, lower lr modestly to 0.03 for stability with the deeper net, and increase epochs to 150 to ensure convergence. This is a conservative but well-grounded capacity+regularization sweep aimed at beating the 5.0 baseline. No architecture script edit is needed since depth/hidden are tunable.

hypothesis.claim: Adding a second hidden layer with wider hidden units (256) plus stronger L2 weight decay (0.0005) will improve generalization and reduce val_top1_err below the 5.0 baseline.
hypothesis.source: L2 weight decay regularization (doi:10.1007/s10462-023-10466-8); ReLU activations to mitigate vanishing gradients (doi:10.1186/s40537-021-00444-8)
hypothesis.predicted_effect: Lower val_top1_err due to increased capacity from depth/width combined with reduced overfitting from stronger weight decay.

reasoning.trace: No experiments have been run yet, so the priority per advisor guidance is to establish a strong baseline with modern training tricks via the available knobs. Among the tunable hyperparameters, the highest-leverage levers for a digits-MLP baseline are depth (deeper enables more representational capacity, supported by ReLU literature mitigating vanishing gradients) and weight_decay (L2 regularization to control overfitting). I'll increase depth from 1 to 2 to add capacity, slightly increase hidden width to 256 for representational power, keep lr moderate, and bump weight_decay to 0.0005 for stronger L2 regularization. Epochs stay at 120 for adequate convergence. This combination targets reducing the current implied error below the 5.0 baseline by combining moderate added capacity with stronger regularization. I avoid extreme settings to stay in a safe, generalizable region since no regions are ruled out yet.

votes.concern: Establishing a baseline with depth and weight_decay is reasonable, but it ignores the strongest literature priors (dropout, batchnorm, data augmentation); follow-up runs should test those higher-leverage knobs rather than just width/depth tweaks.
lr=0.05 may be high for a depth-2 256-wide MLP and could destabilize convergence, but as a first baseline it's a reasonable single config to establish a data point.

Multiple changes (depth, width, weight_decay all at once) are confounded, so a -1.5 win won't tell you which lever mattered — but as the first baseline-establishing run with nothing tried yet, it's a reasonable shot worth taking; just plan to ablate afterward.
